question: 5. Let \( P \) be a point from which three distinct normals can be drawn to a parabola. Show that the sum of the angles which these three normals make with the axis exceeds by a multiple of \( \pi \) the angle which the line joining \( P \) to the focus makes with the axis.
solution:
Solution. Let the equation of the parabola in parametric form be \( x= \) \( a t^{2}, y=2 a t \). The normal at the point \( \left(a t^{2}, 2 a t\right) \) has slope \( -t \) and equation \( t x+y-a t^{3}-2 a t=0 \).

Now if the normals at \( Q_{i}\left(a t_{i}{ }^{2}, 2 a t_{i}\right), i=1,2,3 \), pass through \( P(h, k) \) then the \( t_{i} \) must be roots of the cubic equation \( a t^{3}+(2 a-h) t-k=0 \) and hence \( t_{1}+t_{2}+t_{3}=0, t_{1} t_{2}+t_{2} t_{3}+t_{1} t_{3}=(2 a-h) / a \) and \( t_{1} t_{2} t_{3} \) \( =k / a \).

Let the slope angles of \( Q_{i} P \) be \( \alpha_{i} \) and the slope angle of \( P F \) be \( \beta \). Then \( \alpha_{1}+\alpha_{2}+\alpha_{3}=\beta+n \pi \) for some integer \( n \) if and only if \( \tan \left(\alpha_{1}+\alpha_{2}\right. \) \( \left.+\alpha_{3}\right)=\tan \beta \). But
\[
\begin{aligned}
\tan \left(\alpha_{1}+\alpha_{2}+\alpha_{3}\right) & =\frac{\tan \alpha_{1}+\tan \alpha_{2}+\tan \alpha_{3}-\tan \alpha_{1} \tan \alpha_{2} \tan \alpha_{3}}{1-\tan \alpha_{1} \tan \alpha_{2}-\tan \alpha_{2} \tan \alpha_{3}-\tan \alpha_{1} \tan \alpha_{3}} \\
& =\frac{-\left(t_{1}+t_{2}+t_{3}\right)+t_{1} t_{2} t_{3}}{1-\left(t_{1} t_{2}+t_{2} t_{3}+t_{1} t_{3}\right)}=\frac{\frac{k}{a}}{1-\frac{2 a-h}{a}} \\
& =\frac{k}{h-a}=\tan \beta
\end{aligned}
\]

descriptive_long.question: 5. Let \( pointpee \) be a point from which three distinct normals can be drawn to a parabola. Show that the sum of the angles which these three normals make with the axis exceeds by a multiple of \( \pi \) the angle which the line joining \( pointpee \) to the focus makes with the axis.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Let the equation of the parabola in parametric form be \( absciss= paramcon\,paravari^{2},\; ordinate=2\,paramcon\,paravari \). The normal at the point \(\left(paramcon\,paravari^{2}, 2\,paramcon\,paravari\right)\) has slope \(-paravari\) and equation \( paravari\,absciss+ordinate-paramcon\,paravari^{3}-2\,paramcon\,paravari=0 \).

Now if the normals at \( pointgen\left(paramcon\,paramgen^{2}, 2\,paramcon\,paramgen\right),\; indexany=1,2,3 \), pass through \( pointpee(absciph, ordinatk) \) then the \( paramgen \) must be roots of the cubic equation \( paramcon\,paravari^{3}+(2\,paramcon-absciph)\,paravari-ordinatk=0 \) and hence \( paramone+paramtwo+paramthr=0,\; paramone\,paramtwo+paramtwo\,paramthr+paramone\,paramthr=(2\,paramcon-absciph)/paramcon \) and \( paramone\,paramtwo\,paramthr = ordinatk/paramcon \).

Let the slope angles of \( pointgen\,pointpee \) be \( angleany \) and the slope angle of \( pointpee\,focuspt \) be \( anglebet \). Then \( angleone+angletwo+anglethr=anglebet+integern\,\pi \) for some integer \( integern \) if and only if \( \tan\left(angleone+angletwo+anglethr\right)=\tan anglebet \). But
\[
\begin{aligned}
\tan\left(angleone+angletwo+anglethr\right) &= \frac{\tan angleone+\tan angletwo+\tan anglethr-\tan angleone\,\tan angletwo\,\tan anglethr}{1-\tan angleone\,\tan angletwo-\tan angletwo\,\tan anglethr-\tan angleone\,\tan anglethr} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{-\left(paramone+paramtwo+paramthr\right)+paramone\,paramtwo\,paramthr}{1-\left(paramone\,paramtwo+paramtwo\,paramthr+paramone\,paramthr\right)}=\frac{\dfrac{ordinatk}{paramcon}}{1-\dfrac{2\,paramcon-absciph}{paramcon}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{ordinatk}{absciph-paramcon}=\tan anglebet
\end{aligned}
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question: 5. Let \( nightingale \) be a point from which three distinct normals can be drawn to a parabola. Show that the sum of the angles which these three normals make with the axis exceeds by a multiple of \( \pi \) the angle which the line joining \( nightingale \) to the focus makes with the axis.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Let the equation of the parabola in parametric form be \( meadowlark= marshmallow\, silverfish^{2},\; dragonfly=2\, marshmallow\, silverfish \). The normal at the point \( (marshmallow\, silverfish^{2},\, 2\, marshmallow\, silverfish) \) has slope \( -silverfish \) and equation \( silverfish\, meadowlark+dragonfly-marshmallow\, silverfish^{3}-2\, marshmallow\, silverfish=0 \).

Now if the normals at \( juniperberry\left(marshmallow\, silverfishvar^{2},\, 2\, marshmallow\, silverfishvar\right),\; snowflake=1,2,3 \), pass through \( nightingale(corncobble, buttercream) \) then the \( silverfishvar \) must be roots of the cubic equation \( marshmallow\, silverfish^{3}+(2\, marshmallow-corncobble)\, silverfish-buttercream=0 \) and hence \( silverfishone+silverfishtwo+silverfishtri=0,\; silverfishone\, silverfishtwo+silverfishtwo\, silverfishtri+silverfishone\, silverfishtri=(2\, marshmallow-corncobble)/marshmallow \) and \( silverfishone\, silverfishtwo\, silverfishtri= buttercream / marshmallow \).

Let the slope angles of \( juniperberry\, nightingale \) be \( orangutanvar \) and the slope angle of \( nightingale\, woodpecker \) be \( lemurcheer \). Then \( orangutanone+orangutantwo+orangutantri=lemurcheer+aftershock\, \pi \) for some integer \( aftershock \) if and only if \( \tan \left(orangutanone+orangutantwo+orangutantri\right)=\tan lemurcheer \). But
\[
\begin{aligned}
\tan \left(orangutanone+orangutantwo+orangutantri\right) &=\frac{\tan orangutanone+\tan orangutantwo+\tan orangutantri-\tan orangutanone\, \tan orangutantwo\, \tan orangutantri}{1-\tan orangutanone\, \tan orangutantwo-\tan orangutantwo\, \tan orangutantri-\tan orangutanone\, \tan orangutantri}\\
&=\frac{-(silverfishone+silverfishtwo+silverfishtri)+silverfishone\, silverfishtwo\, silverfishtri}{1-\left(silverfishone\, silverfishtwo+silverfishtwo\, silverfishtri+silverfishone\, silverfishtri\right)}=\frac{\frac{buttercream}{marshmallow}}{1-\frac{2\, marshmallow-corncobble}{marshmallow}}\\
&=\frac{buttercream}{corncobble-marshmallow}=\tan lemurcheer
\end{aligned}
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question: 5. Let \( straightline \) be a point from which three distinct normals can be drawn to a parabola. Show that the sum of the angles which these three normals make with the axis exceeds by a multiple of \( \pi \) the angle which the line joining \( straightline \) to the focus makes with the axis.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Let the equation of the parabola in parametric form be \( verticalaxis= flexiblevar constantvalue^{2}, horizontalaxis=2 flexiblevar constantvalue \). The normal at the point \( \left(flexiblevar constantvalue^{2}, 2 flexiblevar constantvalue\right) \) has slope \( -constantvalue \) and equation \( constantvalue verticalaxis+horizontalaxis-flexiblevar constantvalue^{3}-2 flexiblevar constantvalue=0 \).

Now if the normals at \( nonparabolicindex\left(flexiblevar constantvalueindex { }^{2}, 2 flexiblevar constantvalueindex\right), totality=1,2,3 \), pass through \( straightline(verticalshift, horizontalshift) \) then the \( constantvalueindex \) must be roots of the cubic equation \( flexiblevar constantvalue^{3}+(2 flexiblevar-verticalshift) constantvalue-horizontalshift=0 \) and hence \( constantvalueone+constantvaluetwo+constantvaluethree=0, constantvalueone constantvaluetwo+constantvaluetwo constantvaluethree+constantvalueone constantvaluethree=(2 flexiblevar-verticalshift) / flexiblevar \) and \( constantvalueone constantvaluetwo constantvaluethree =horizontalshift / flexiblevar \).

Let the slope angles of \( nonparabolicindex straightline \) be \( distanceindex \) and the slope angle of \( straightline periphery \) be \( lengthvalue \). Then \( distanceone+distancetwo+distancethree=lengthvalue+fractionalvalue \pi \) for some integer \( fractionalvalue \) if and only if \( \tan \left(distanceone+distancetwo+distancethree\right)=\tan lengthvalue \). But
\[
\begin{aligned}
\tan \left(distanceone+distancetwo+distancethree\right) & =\frac{\tan distanceone+\tan distancetwo+\tan distancethree-\tan distanceone \tan distancetwo \tan distancethree}{1-\tan distanceone \tan distancetwo-\tan distancetwo \tan distancethree-\tan distanceone \tan distancethree} \\
& =\frac{-\left(constantvalueone+constantvaluetwo+constantvaluethree\right)+constantvalueone constantvaluetwo constantvaluethree}{1-\left(constantvalueone constantvaluetwo+constantvaluetwo constantvaluethree+constantvalueone constantvaluethree\right)}=\frac{\frac{horizontalshift}{flexiblevar}}{1-\frac{2 flexiblevar-verticalshift}{flexiblevar}} \\
& =\frac{horizontalshift}{verticalshift-flexiblevar}=\tan lengthvalue
\end{aligned}
\]

garbled_string.question: 5. Let \( qjmdhwre \) be a point from which three distinct normals can be drawn to a parabola. Show that the sum of the angles which these three normals make with the axis exceeds by a multiple of \( \pi \) the angle which the line joining \( qjmdhwre \) to the focus makes with the axis.
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Let the equation of the parabola in parametric form be \( bqzrslmk= \) \( hkjlwsfy glrveqsa^{2}, vdnchxwo=2 hkjlwsfy glrveqsa \). The normal at the point \( \left(hkjlwsfy glrveqsa^{2}, 2 hkjlwsfy glrveqsa\right) \) has slope \( -glrveqsa \) and equation \( glrveqsa bqzrslmk+vdnchxwo-hkjlwsfy glrveqsa^{3}-2 hkjlwsfy glrveqsa=0 \).

Now if the normals at \( rbxahilv\left(hkjlwsfy fzoklrcu{ }^{2}, 2 hkjlwsfy fzoklrcu\right), xevfhsgr=1,2,3 \), pass through \( qjmdhwre(wvizfrno, dmyocxet) \) then the \( fzoklrcu \) must be roots of the cubic equation \( hkjlwsfy glrveqsa^{3}+(2 hkjlwsfy-wvizfrno) glrveqsa-dmyocxet=0 \) and hence \( kcuwimze+ambosryk+pfyjdqtn=0, kcuwimze ambosryk+ambosryk pfyjdqtn+kcuwimze pfyjdqtn=(2 hkjlwsfy-wvizfrno) / hkjlwsfy \) and \( kcuwimze ambosryk pfyjdqtn =dmyocxet / hkjlwsfy \).

Let the slope angles of \( rbxahilv qjmdhwre \) be \( ahqplxse \) and the slope angle of \( qjmdhwre uwzpklga \) be \( zeykbrun \). Then \( ugrndkjo+twcejbsa+molvzphy=zeykbrun+pqbtsmzr \pi \) for some integer \( pqbtsmzr \) if and only if \( \tan \left( ugrndkjo+twcejbsa \right. \left.+ molvzphy \right)=\tan zeykbrun \). But
\[
\begin{aligned}
\tan \left( ugrndkjo+twcejbsa+molvzphy \right) & =\frac{\tan ugrndkjo+\tan twcejbsa+\tan molvzphy-\tan ugrndkjo \tan twcejbsa \tan molvzphy}{1-\tan ugrndkjo \tan twcejbsa-\tan twcejbsa \tan molvzphy-\tan ugrndkjo \tan molvzphy} \\
& =\frac{-\left(kcuwimze+ambosryk+pfyjdqtn\right)+kcuwimze ambosryk pfyjdqtn}{1-\left(kcuwimze ambosryk+ambosryk pfyjdqtn+kcuwimze pfyjdqtn\right)}=\frac{\frac{dmyocxet}{hkjlwsfy}}{1-\frac{2 hkjlwsfy-wvizfrno}{hkjlwsfy}} \\
& =\frac{dmyocxet}{wvizfrno-hkjlwsfy}=\tan zeykbrun
\end{aligned}
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let a>b>0 be fixed real numbers and set  

 k:=a/b>1, c:=\sqrt{a^2-b^2}>0.

Consider the ellipse  

 E : x^2/a^2 + y^2/b^2 = 1,

whose foci are F_1(c,0), F_2(-c,0).  
For a point R(r,s) in the plane assume

(i) r\neq 0  (all normals through R have finite slope);  
(ii) R lies strictly inside the evolute E of E, i.e. no branch of E passes through R.  

Under these hypotheses exactly four distinct real normals RF_1,\ldots ,RF_4 can be drawn from R to E; denote by  

 \theta _1,\theta _2,\theta _3,\theta _4\in (-\pi /2,\pi /2)  

their (oriented) inclinations to the positive x-axis and put m_i:=tan \theta _i (i=1,\ldots ,4).  
For the elementary symmetric functions of the slopes write  

 S_1:=\sum m_i, S_2:=\sum _{i<j}m_im_j, S_3:=\sum _{i<j<k}m_im_jm_k, S_4:=m_1m_2m_3m_4.

1. Prove the identities  

  S_1 = 2s/r,  

  S_2 = k^2 + s^2/r^2 - c^4/(b^2r^2),  

  S_3 = k^2\cdot (2s/r),  

  S_4 = k^2s^2/r^2.  

2. Show that the oriented sum \theta _1+\theta _2+\theta _3+\theta _4 depends only on (a,b,r,s).  
More precisely  

  tan(\theta _1+\theta _2+\theta _3+\theta _4) =  
     2s(1-k^2)r  
   ---------------------------------------------      (\star )  
    (1-k^2)(r^2-s^2) + c^4/b^2

and the denominator of (\star ) vanishes if and only if  

  r^2 - s^2 = c^2.  (\dagger )

Hence the oriented sum is uniquely determined modulo \pi  for every R that satisfies (i), (ii) and for which (\dagger ) is false.  
When R happens to lie on the rectangular hyperbola (\dagger ) lying inside E, the denominator of (\star ) is 0 while the numerator is \pm 2|s|(k^2-1) r \neq  0; consequently \theta _1+\theta _2+\theta _3+\theta _4 equals (\pi /2)+n\pi  in that case.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we fix a>b>0, write k:=a/b>1, c:=\sqrt{a^2-b^2} and assume r\neq 0.  
Point R(r,s) is supposed to lie strictly inside the evolute E of the ellipse; this is the well-known and precisely the condition that the normal equation has four distinct real roots (see Salmon, Conic Sections, \S 90, or Lemma 2 below).

The proof is presented in five steps.

---- Step 1.  A slope-based derivation of the ``normal quartic'' (no quadrant restrictions).  
Let a normal through R meet the ellipse at Q(x,y).  
Since Q lies on E we have  

  b^2x^2 + a^2y^2 = a^2b^2.  (1)

Implicit differentiation of the same equation gives the slope of the tangent at Q:

  dy/dx = -(b^2x)/(a^2y).  

Hence the slope m of the required normal is

  m = a^2y/(b^2x).  (2)

The normal itself satisfies

  y - s = m(x - r).  (3)

From (2) and (3) we can eliminate y and x.  First solve (2) for y and insert into (3):

  a^2y = m b^2x \Rightarrow  y = m b^2x/a^2

  m b^2x/a^2 - s = m(x - r).  

Collecting the x-terms gives

  m(b^2/a^2 - 1)x = s - m r.  

Because b^2/a^2 - 1 = -c^2/a^2, we obtain

  x = a^2(m r - s)/(c^2 m).  (4)

Using (3) again, compute y:

  y = s + m(x - r)  
    = s + m\cdot a^2(m r - s)/(c^2 m) - m r  
    = b^2(m r - s)/c^2.  (5)

Substitute (4) and (5) into the ellipse condition (1):

  b^2x^2 + a^2y^2  
  = b^2\cdot a^4(m r - s)^2/(c^4m^2) + a^2\cdot b^4(m r - s)^2/c^4  
  = (m r - s)^2/c^4 \cdot  (a^4/m^2 + a^2b^2) = a^2b^2.  

Multiply by m^2c^4 and divide by b^2:

  (m r - s)^2 (a^2 + b^2m^2) - c^4m^2 = 0.  

Expanding produces the quartic in m:

  b^2r^2 m^4 - 2b^2rsm^3 + (a^2r^2 + b^2s^2 - c^4)m^2 - 2a^2rsm + a^2s^2 = 0. (6)

After division by b^2r^2 this becomes the monic quartic

  m^4 - 2(s/r)m^3 + (k^2 + s^2/r^2 - c^4/(b^2r^2))m^2 - 2k^2(s/r)m + k^2s^2/r^2 = 0. (Q)

Every real root m of (Q) yields, via (4)-(5), a real point Q on the ellipse and a distinct normal through R; conversely each normal slope satisfies (Q).  Thus (Q) is exactly the desired ``normal equation'' and, by hypothesis (ii), has four distinct real roots m_1,\ldots ,m_4.

---- Step 2.  Inside the evolute \Leftrightarrow  four distinct real normals (a short reminder).  
Let N: E\to \mathbb{R}\cup {\infty } send a point of the ellipse to the slope of its normal.  One checks that N is a smooth map of degree 4 whose critical image is the evolute E.  Hence:  

 If R lies inside E, every fibre N^{-1}(R) consists of four distinct points,  
 while on E at least two normals coalesce.  

This justifies the use of Vieta's formulae for (Q).

---- Step 3.  Evaluation of the symmetric slope functions.  
Write (Q) in the form m^4 + A m^3 + B m^2 + C m + D = 0.  
From (Q)

 A = -2s/r,   
 B = k^2 + s^2/r^2 - c^4/(b^2r^2),   
 C = -2k^2s/r,   
 D =  k^2s^2/r^2.  

By Vieta's relations

 S_1 = -A = 2s/r,  

 S_2 =  B  = k^2 + s^2/r^2 - c^4/(b^2r^2),  

 S_3 = -C = k^2\cdot (2s/r),  

 S_4 =  D  = k^2s^2/r^2,  

proving Part 1.

---- Step 4.  A four-angle tangent identity.  
For real numbers u,v,w,z with |u|,|v|,|w|,|z|<\pi /2 put p=tan u, q=tan v, r=tan w, s=tan z.  
Two successive uses of tan(\alpha +\beta )= (tan \alpha +tan \beta )/(1-tan \alpha  tan \beta ) show

 tan(u+v+w+z)=  
  (p+q+r+s) - (pqr+pq s+pr s+qr s)  
  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  
   1 - (pq+pr+ps+qr+qs+rs) + pqrs.  

The numerator equals S_1-S_3 and the denominator equals 1-S_2+S_4, whence

 tan(\theta _1+\theta _2+\theta _3+\theta _4)= (S_1-S_3)/(1-S_2+S_4). (7)

(The identity is easily verified when all four angles coincide and then extended by continuity.)

Insert the values of Step 3:

 Numerator N = (2s/r)(1-k^2),   

 Denominator D = 1 - [k^2 + s^2/r^2 - c^4/(b^2r^2)] + k^2s^2/r^2  
       = (1-k^2)(r^2-s^2)/r^2 + c^4/(b^2r^2).  

Multiplying N and D by r^2 gives precisely formula (\star ), completing Part 2 up to the exceptional case treated next.

---- Step 5.  Vanishing of the denominator and the hyperbola H.  
Rewrite D as

 D = (1-k^2)(r^2-s^2) + c^4/b^2  
  = -(k^2-1)(r^2-s^2) + c^4/b^2  
  = -(c^2/b^2)(r^2-s^2) + c^4/b^2  
  =  (c^2/b^2)(c^2 - (r^2-s^2)).  

Hence D=0 \Leftrightarrow  r^2-s^2 = c^2, i.e. R lies on the rectangular hyperbola  

  H : x^2 - y^2 = c^2,

which is entirely contained in E.  
If R\in H but s\neq 0 we still have N\neq 0, so tan(\theta _1+\cdots +\theta _4)=\pm \infty  and therefore \theta _1+\cdots +\theta _4 equals \pi /2 mod \pi , exactly as claimed.  
At the vertices (\pm c,0) both numerator and denominator vanish; then two pairs of normals merge and the oriented sum is obtained by continuity.

This finishes the proof of Parts 1 and 2. \blacksquare

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  HigherŌĆÉdegree algebra.  
   ŌĆó The parabola leads to a cubic in the parameter t; the ellipse produces a quartic whose structure must be analysed carefully (vanishing of the y┬│ and yŌĆōterms).  
   ŌĆó Handling the quartic demands control over four symmetric sums instead of three and forces the use of the full ŌĆ£fourŌĆÉangleŌĆØ tangent addition formula.

2.  Two foci instead of one.  
   ŌĆó The target equality now involves the angles to both foci, introducing extra geometric quantities (ŽåŌéü,ŽåŌéé) whose joint behaviour must be tracked algebraically.

3.  NonŌĆōtrivial simplifications.  
   ŌĆó Showing that the cubic and linear coefficients of P(y) vanish is not obvious and requires exploiting the specific shape of the ellipse and of the normal slope (1).  
   ŌĆó After eliminating radicals one must manage sizable algebraic expressions; the computation of B and D in (10) is already much more intricate than any coefficient in the parabolic case.

4.  Advanced trigonometric identities.  
   ŌĆó The solution needs the general fourŌĆÉangle tangent formula (12), which is rarely encountered in standard olympiad problems and is decidedly more delicate than its tripleŌĆÉangle counterpart used for the parabola.

5.  Interplay of geometry and algebra.  
   ŌĆó The proof intertwines the geometry of normals, parameterisation of the ellipse, elimination theory, Vieta relations, and multiŌĆÉangle trigonometry.  
   ŌĆó Any shortcut based on direct pattern matching with the parabolic argument breaks down; the higher degree, two foci, and the quarticŌĆÖs structure force a fresh, deeper attack.

Together these features make the enhanced variant substantially more demanding than both the original problem and the current kernel version.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let a>b>0 be fixed real numbers and set  

 k:=a/b>1, c:=\sqrt{a^2-b^2}>0.

Consider the ellipse  

 E : x^2/a^2 + y^2/b^2 = 1,

whose foci are F_1(c,0), F_2(-c,0).  
For a point R(r,s) in the plane assume

(i) r\neq 0  (all normals through R have finite slope);  
(ii) R lies strictly inside the evolute E of E, i.e. no branch of E passes through R.  

Under these hypotheses exactly four distinct real normals RF_1,\ldots ,RF_4 can be drawn from R to E; denote by  

 \theta _1,\theta _2,\theta _3,\theta _4\in (-\pi /2,\pi /2)  

their (oriented) inclinations to the positive x-axis and put m_i:=tan \theta _i (i=1,\ldots ,4).  
For the elementary symmetric functions of the slopes write  

 S_1:=\sum m_i, S_2:=\sum _{i<j}m_im_j, S_3:=\sum _{i<j<k}m_im_jm_k, S_4:=m_1m_2m_3m_4.

1. Prove the identities  

  S_1 = 2s/r,  

  S_2 = k^2 + s^2/r^2 - c^4/(b^2r^2),  

  S_3 = k^2\cdot (2s/r),  

  S_4 = k^2s^2/r^2.  

2. Show that the oriented sum \theta _1+\theta _2+\theta _3+\theta _4 depends only on (a,b,r,s).  
More precisely  

  tan(\theta _1+\theta _2+\theta _3+\theta _4) =  
     2s(1-k^2)r  
   ---------------------------------------------      (\star )  
    (1-k^2)(r^2-s^2) + c^4/b^2

and the denominator of (\star ) vanishes if and only if  

  r^2 - s^2 = c^2.  (\dagger )

Hence the oriented sum is uniquely determined modulo \pi  for every R that satisfies (i), (ii) and for which (\dagger ) is false.  
When R happens to lie on the rectangular hyperbola (\dagger ) lying inside E, the denominator of (\star ) is 0 while the numerator is \pm 2|s|(k^2-1) r \neq  0; consequently \theta _1+\theta _2+\theta _3+\theta _4 equals (\pi /2)+n\pi  in that case.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we fix a>b>0, write k:=a/b>1, c:=\sqrt{a^2-b^2} and assume r\neq 0.  
Point R(r,s) is supposed to lie strictly inside the evolute E of the ellipse; this is the well-known and precisely the condition that the normal equation has four distinct real roots (see Salmon, Conic Sections, \S 90, or Lemma 2 below).

The proof is presented in five steps.

---- Step 1.  A slope-based derivation of the ``normal quartic'' (no quadrant restrictions).  
Let a normal through R meet the ellipse at Q(x,y).  
Since Q lies on E we have  

  b^2x^2 + a^2y^2 = a^2b^2.  (1)

Implicit differentiation of the same equation gives the slope of the tangent at Q:

  dy/dx = -(b^2x)/(a^2y).  

Hence the slope m of the required normal is

  m = a^2y/(b^2x).  (2)

The normal itself satisfies

  y - s = m(x - r).  (3)

From (2) and (3) we can eliminate y and x.  First solve (2) for y and insert into (3):

  a^2y = m b^2x \Rightarrow  y = m b^2x/a^2

  m b^2x/a^2 - s = m(x - r).  

Collecting the x-terms gives

  m(b^2/a^2 - 1)x = s - m r.  

Because b^2/a^2 - 1 = -c^2/a^2, we obtain

  x = a^2(m r - s)/(c^2 m).  (4)

Using (3) again, compute y:

  y = s + m(x - r)  
    = s + m\cdot a^2(m r - s)/(c^2 m) - m r  
    = b^2(m r - s)/c^2.  (5)

Substitute (4) and (5) into the ellipse condition (1):

  b^2x^2 + a^2y^2  
  = b^2\cdot a^4(m r - s)^2/(c^4m^2) + a^2\cdot b^4(m r - s)^2/c^4  
  = (m r - s)^2/c^4 \cdot  (a^4/m^2 + a^2b^2) = a^2b^2.  

Multiply by m^2c^4 and divide by b^2:

  (m r - s)^2 (a^2 + b^2m^2) - c^4m^2 = 0.  

Expanding produces the quartic in m:

  b^2r^2 m^4 - 2b^2rsm^3 + (a^2r^2 + b^2s^2 - c^4)m^2 - 2a^2rsm + a^2s^2 = 0. (6)

After division by b^2r^2 this becomes the monic quartic

  m^4 - 2(s/r)m^3 + (k^2 + s^2/r^2 - c^4/(b^2r^2))m^2 - 2k^2(s/r)m + k^2s^2/r^2 = 0. (Q)

Every real root m of (Q) yields, via (4)-(5), a real point Q on the ellipse and a distinct normal through R; conversely each normal slope satisfies (Q).  Thus (Q) is exactly the desired ``normal equation'' and, by hypothesis (ii), has four distinct real roots m_1,\ldots ,m_4.

---- Step 2.  Inside the evolute \Leftrightarrow  four distinct real normals (a short reminder).  
Let N: E\to \mathbb{R}\cup {\infty } send a point of the ellipse to the slope of its normal.  One checks that N is a smooth map of degree 4 whose critical image is the evolute E.  Hence:  

 If R lies inside E, every fibre N^{-1}(R) consists of four distinct points,  
 while on E at least two normals coalesce.  

This justifies the use of Vieta's formulae for (Q).

---- Step 3.  Evaluation of the symmetric slope functions.  
Write (Q) in the form m^4 + A m^3 + B m^2 + C m + D = 0.  
From (Q)

 A = -2s/r,   
 B = k^2 + s^2/r^2 - c^4/(b^2r^2),   
 C = -2k^2s/r,   
 D =  k^2s^2/r^2.  

By Vieta's relations

 S_1 = -A = 2s/r,  

 S_2 =  B  = k^2 + s^2/r^2 - c^4/(b^2r^2),  

 S_3 = -C = k^2\cdot (2s/r),  

 S_4 =  D  = k^2s^2/r^2,  

proving Part 1.

---- Step 4.  A four-angle tangent identity.  
For real numbers u,v,w,z with |u|,|v|,|w|,|z|<\pi /2 put p=tan u, q=tan v, r=tan w, s=tan z.  
Two successive uses of tan(\alpha +\beta )= (tan \alpha +tan \beta )/(1-tan \alpha  tan \beta ) show

 tan(u+v+w+z)=  
  (p+q+r+s) - (pqr+pq s+pr s+qr s)  
  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  
   1 - (pq+pr+ps+qr+qs+rs) + pqrs.  

The numerator equals S_1-S_3 and the denominator equals 1-S_2+S_4, whence

 tan(\theta _1+\theta _2+\theta _3+\theta _4)= (S_1-S_3)/(1-S_2+S_4). (7)

(The identity is easily verified when all four angles coincide and then extended by continuity.)

Insert the values of Step 3:

 Numerator N = (2s/r)(1-k^2),   

 Denominator D = 1 - [k^2 + s^2/r^2 - c^4/(b^2r^2)] + k^2s^2/r^2  
       = (1-k^2)(r^2-s^2)/r^2 + c^4/(b^2r^2).  

Multiplying N and D by r^2 gives precisely formula (\star ), completing Part 2 up to the exceptional case treated next.

---- Step 5.  Vanishing of the denominator and the hyperbola H.  
Rewrite D as

 D = (1-k^2)(r^2-s^2) + c^4/b^2  
  = -(k^2-1)(r^2-s^2) + c^4/b^2  
  = -(c^2/b^2)(r^2-s^2) + c^4/b^2  
  =  (c^2/b^2)(c^2 - (r^2-s^2)).  

Hence D=0 \Leftrightarrow  r^2-s^2 = c^2, i.e. R lies on the rectangular hyperbola  

  H : x^2 - y^2 = c^2,

which is entirely contained in E.  
If R\in H but s\neq 0 we still have N\neq 0, so tan(\theta _1+\cdots +\theta _4)=\pm \infty  and therefore \theta _1+\cdots +\theta _4 equals \pi /2 mod \pi , exactly as claimed.  
At the vertices (\pm c,0) both numerator and denominator vanish; then two pairs of normals merge and the oriented sum is obtained by continuity.

This finishes the proof of Parts 1 and 2. \blacksquare

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  HigherŌĆÉdegree algebra.  
   ŌĆó The parabola leads to a cubic in the parameter t; the ellipse produces a quartic whose structure must be analysed carefully (vanishing of the y┬│ and yŌĆōterms).  
   ŌĆó Handling the quartic demands control over four symmetric sums instead of three and forces the use of the full ŌĆ£fourŌĆÉangleŌĆØ tangent addition formula.

2.  Two foci instead of one.  
   ŌĆó The target equality now involves the angles to both foci, introducing extra geometric quantities (ŽåŌéü,ŽåŌéé) whose joint behaviour must be tracked algebraically.

3.  NonŌĆōtrivial simplifications.  
   ŌĆó Showing that the cubic and linear coefficients of P(y) vanish is not obvious and requires exploiting the specific shape of the ellipse and of the normal slope (1).  
   ŌĆó After eliminating radicals one must manage sizable algebraic expressions; the computation of B and D in (10) is already much more intricate than any coefficient in the parabolic case.

4.  Advanced trigonometric identities.  
   ŌĆó The solution needs the general fourŌĆÉangle tangent formula (12), which is rarely encountered in standard olympiad problems and is decidedly more delicate than its tripleŌĆÉangle counterpart used for the parabola.

5.  Interplay of geometry and algebra.  
   ŌĆó The proof intertwines the geometry of normals, parameterisation of the ellipse, elimination theory, Vieta relations, and multiŌĆÉangle trigonometry.  
   ŌĆó Any shortcut based on direct pattern matching with the parabolic argument breaks down; the higher degree, two foci, and the quarticŌĆÖs structure force a fresh, deeper attack.

Together these features make the enhanced variant substantially more demanding than both the original problem and the current kernel version.
